target.title: fix(discord): deliver reasoning replies
target.body:
Summary:
- Allow Discord to deliver `isReasoning` block/final payloads instead of dropping them in the message delivery hooks.
- Format opted-in Discord reasoning replies as visible `Thinking` payloads and clear the internal `isReasoning` marker before shared durable outbound delivery.
- Add regression coverage for both the Discord message-handler boundary and the durable reply-delivery boundary.

Verification:
- `git diff --check`
- `pnpm dlx oxfmt@0.52.0 --check --threads=1 extensions/discord/src/monitor/message-handler.process.ts extensions/discord/src/monitor/message-handler.process.test.ts extensions/discord/src/monitor/message-handler.process.abort-skip.test.ts extensions/discord/src/monitor/reply-delivery.ts extensions/discord/src/monitor/reply-delivery.test.ts`
- Blacksmith Testbox `tbx_01kvgcs8zt910xtwaqyp8mc89g`, run `https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/actions/runs/27838363510`: `corepack pnpm test:serial extensions/discord/src/monitor/message-handler.process.test.ts extensions/discord/src/monitor/reply-delivery.test.ts src/infra/outbound/payloads.test.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-subscribe.subscribe-embedded-agent-session.emits-reasoning-as-separate-message-enabled.test.ts` passed. Shards: outbound payloads 36 tests; agent reasoning 8 tests; Discord handler/delivery 124 tests.

Fixes #94936

target.title: fix(feishu): use sendReplyToMessageId in streaming card start() for DM chats
target.body:
Fixes #94922

## What

Change the streaming card `start()` call in `createFeishuReplyDispatcher` to use `sendReplyToMessageId` instead of the raw `replyToMessageId`.

## Why

In `reply-dispatcher.ts:395`, the streaming card's `start()` call received `replyToMessageId` (the raw value from the inbound message) instead of `sendReplyToMessageId` (which is `undefined` for DM chats when `skipReplyToInMessages` is true). This meant that even in 1:1 DM conversations, the streaming card was created as a reply to the user's message, causing:

1. A "Reply to [User]" label displayed in the Feishu client
2. Slower message delivery due to reply vs. direct send API overhead

All other send paths (text, media, structured card) already used `sendReplyToMessageId`. The streaming card was the only outlier.

The fix aligns the streaming card path with every other delivery path in the dispatcher, ensuring `skipReplyToInMessages` is respected consistently.

## Root cause

The `skipReplyToInMessages` guard (line 173) computes `sendReplyToMessageId` as `undefined` when `!isGroup && !directThreadReply` (DM sessions). This correctly suppresses reply metadata for plain-text, media, and structured-card sends. But the streaming card `start()` bypassed this guard by using the raw `replyToMessageId` directly, so DM streaming cards always fell into `resolveStreamingCardSendMode` → `"reply"` → `im.message.reply` API → visible "Reply to" label.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed:** Feishu streaming card sends messages with `replyToMessageId` in DM chats, causing "Reply to [User]" label
- **Environment tested:** OpenClaw main branch (27f702d6) and PR branch (651f77f0), Node.js, Feishu extension
- **Exact steps or command run after the patch:** Checked the dispatcher source to confirm `sendReplyToMessageId` is now used consistently at the streaming card start path, and ran the reply-dispatcher verification suite (all scenarios including the new DM streaming card scenario pass)

```
$ grep -n "sendReplyToMessageId" extensions/feishu/src/reply-dispatcher.ts | head -10
173:  const sendReplyToMessageId = skipReplyToInMessages ? undefined : replyToMessageId;
395:          replyToMessageId: sendReplyToMessageId,
523:          replyToMessageId: sendReplyToMessageId,
540:                replyToMessageId: sendReplyToMessageId,
567:                    replyToMessageId: sendReplyToMessageId,
593:      replyToMessageId: sendReplyToMessageId,
765:                  replyToMessageId: sendReplyToMessageId,
784:                  replyToMessageId: sendReplyToMessageId,
```

```
$ node -e "const fs=require('fs'); const c=fs.readFileSync('extensions/feishu/src/reply-dispatcher.ts','utf8'); const lines=c.split('\n'); console.log('Line 395:', lines[394].trim()); console.log('All sendReplyToMessageId refs:', (c.match(/sendReplyToMessageId/g)||[]).length); console.log('skipReplyToInMessages guard:', c.includes('skipReplyToInMessages ? undefined : replyToMessageId'));"
Line 395: replyToMessageId: sendReplyToMessageId,
All sendReplyToMessageId refs: 9
skipReplyToInMessages guard: true
```

- **After-fix evidence:** Line 395 now uses `sendReplyToMessageId` consistently with all other send paths (lines 523, 540, 567, 593, 765, 784). The streaming card respects the `skipReplyToInMessages` guard, so DM chats no longer get `replyToMessageId` set. Terminal output above confirms the fix is applied and the guard is present. When `sendReplyToMessageId` is `undefined` (DM with `skipReplyToInMessages=true`), `resolveStreamingCardSendMode` returns `"create"` mode → `im.message.create` (no Reply-to label). When present (group/thread), it returns `"reply"` mode → `im.message.reply` (correct threading).
- **Observed result after fix:** All 9 `sendReplyToMessageId` references in `reply-dispatcher.ts` now use the DM-aware value. The streaming card start (line 395) is no longer the sole outlier using the raw `replyToMessageId`. P2P direct-thread replies are preserved because `skipReplyToInMessages` keeps `sendReplyToMessageId` set for `directThreadReply` scenarios. All reply-dispatcher scenarios pass.
- **What was not tested:** Live Feishu DM delivery (requires Feishu bot credentials and a real DM conversation). The production function `resolveStreamingCardSendMode` was verified by source inspection, which is the exact function that determines the API call path based on the `replyToMessageId` value passed to `streaming.start()`.

Co-Authored-By: Claude <noreply@anthropic.com>

target.title: fix(whatsapp): add listGroupsLive for real-time group directory
target.body:
## Root Cause

The WhatsApp channel directory adapter only registered `listGroups` (which reads from the config map `channels.whatsapp.groups`) but did **not** register `listGroupsLive`. The CLI (and other consumers) prefer `listGroupsLive` when available, so users who have active WhatsApp group memberships but an empty config-based groups map would see an empty directory. No `listGroupsLive` implementation existed for WhatsApp.

## Summary

- Bug: WhatsApp directory `/groups` returned empty even when the account is a member of groups, because only the config-backed `listGroups` was wired up but not the live-fetch variant.
- This PR adds `listWhatsAppDirectoryGroupsLive` that fetches groups from the Baileys socket via `groupFetchAllParticipating()`, with graceful fallback to config when the socket is unavailable or throws. The new function is registered in the channel directory adapter.
- Fixes #76722

## Revision (2026-06-20)

- Fixed a P2 issue where live groups were filtered by JID only, so `--query "Family"` would not match a group named "Family Chat". Now builds entries first and filters by both JID and subject name (matching Discord/Slack live directory behavior).
- Commit: `e7ac40ee0e`

## Verification

- `pnpm test extensions/whatsapp/src/directory-config.test.ts` — 8/8 passed (6 new tests + 2 existing; +1 test for name-based filtering)
- `pnpm tsgo:test` — passed (type check)
- `pnpm lint:extensions:bundled` — passed
- Autoreview: clean, no accepted/actionable findings
- CI: The `checks-node-core-tooling` failure is an infrastructure/flaky issue unrelated to this PR (only touches `extensions/whatsapp/`)

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: WhatsApp directory `/groups` now returns the account's real group memberships fetched live from the WhatsApp connection, filtered by both JID and group name.
Real environment tested: Linux, Node 22, branch `fix/issue-76722-whatsapp-directory-groups-live` commit `e7ac40ee0e`
Exact steps or command run after this patch:
```bash
pnpm test extensions/whatsapp/src/directory-config.test.ts
pnpm tsgo:test
pnpm lint:extensions:bundled
```
Evidence after fix:
```console
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/whatsapp/src/directory-config.test.ts

 RUN  v4.1.8

 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  8 passed (8)
   Start at  00:50:52
   Duration  25.63s
```
Observed result after fix: All 8 tests pass including the new test for name-based query filtering. Type check and lint pass cleanly.
What was not tested: Live E2E with a real WhatsApp account (requires Crabbox with WhatsApp auth); no WhatsApp directory binary/E2E test infrastructure exists.

target.title: feat: channel echo / session pinning
target.body: ## Pin-from-here: channel-agnostic turn mirroring A session can mirror its turns to other threads bound to the **same** session. You run `/pin on` in a thread; from then on, when any other thread of that session drives a turn, this thread receives it — rendered **natively through its own channel's dispatch**, persisted as that channel's own turn. ### Authorization (the rework) The original revision let an operator add an echo target to an arbitrary chat id; the maintainer flagged that as streaming session content to any chat. This revision removes that surface entirely: - **`/pin`** is in-chat, opt-in, and binds only the session already bound to *this* thread — every mirror recipient is, by construction, an already-authorized participant of that session. There is no "stream to an arbitrary chat id" path. - Operator echo adds require a session participant (`targetMatchesSessionParticipant`), fail-closed. ### How the mirror renders — channel-agnostic, by reuse not reimplementation The agent runs **once**; its parsed event stream is broadcast on the agent-event bus keyed by `runId`. For each pinned target, the core hands that target's **own channel dispatch** a bus-sourced `replyResolver` (`createMirrorReplyResolver`) that replays the origin run's stream instead of calling the model: - `dispatchInboundMessageWithDispatcher` already accepts a pluggable `replyResolver`. `launchMirrorDispatch` (core, channel-agnostic) drives each target through its registered mirror dispatcher with that resolver. - Telegram registers a thin `dispatchMirror` that synthesizes an inbound for the pinned chat/thread and runs its **existing** `dispatchTelegramMessage` with the resolver. So drafts, streaming, formatting, and persistence all follow **that account's own config** — streaming-capable + on → the mirror streams; off → final only. Nothing is forced; nothing is reimplemented per channel. - **Loop-safe by construction:** the `replyResolver` bypasses the agent-run path, so a mirror turn never re-enters the mirror fan-out. The post-hoc final echo skips any target a mirror turn rendered (handled-mark dedup) so there's no double delivery. This replaces the earlier per-channel streaming-echo *renderer* (telegram-only, reimplemented rendering) with one small channel-agnostic mechanism that reuses each channel's pipeline. **Net −894 LOC**: deletes `echo-renderer*`, `echo-streaming`, `streaming-progress-callbacks`, the `registerEchoRendererFactory` plugin-api plumbing, and the `plugin-sdk/channel-echo` subpath; adds a small core launcher/registry (reusing the existing `echo-mirror-resolver`) plus a thin telegram adapter. ### Real behavior proof (Telegram-desktop, mantis-equivalent) - **Behavior or issue addressed**: Pin-from-here mirrors a session turn into pinned target threads by re-running each target channel's own dispatch with a bus-sourced reply resolver, so the mirror renders + persists natively (streaming/verbose per that channel's own config) instead of a flat post-hoc echo. - **Real environment tested**: A real OpenClaw gateway built from this branch in a Linux crabbox with a desktop; a real Telegram bot + a real Telegram account on Telegram Desktop; a real `claude-cli` backend (`sonnet-4-6`/`opus-4-8`, live Anthropic API) with exec tools; the tdlib user-driver sending real messages. This recreates the Mantis telegram-desktop proof flow locally (same mechanism: telegram-desktop + user-driver + screen capture). - **Exact steps or command run after this patch**: Started the gateway from the built branch; in a Telegram thread sent `/new`, `/verbose on`, `/pin on`; then drove the same session from webchat (`chat.send`) with a tool-using prompt (`sleep 3; hostname`, and a two-step `date -u` + `uname -a` narrated before each command); screen-recorded Telegram Desktop and captured the gateway debug log. - **Evidence after fix**: Direct after-fix artifacts — mirror-render screenshot [`.proof/pin-from-here-mirror-dispatch.png`](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anagnorisis2peripeteia/openclaw/proof/pin-from-here-evidence/.proof/pin-from-here-mirror-dispatch.png), multi-account screenshot [`.proof/pin-from-here-multiaccount.png`](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anagnorisis2peripeteia/openclaw/proof/pin-from-here-evidence/.proof/pin-from-here-multiaccount.png), and the full **destination-revocation run + gateway log** [`.proof/pin-from-here-revocation.txt`](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anagnorisis2peripeteia/openclaw/proof/pin-from-here-evidence/.proof/pin-from-here-revocation.txt). The revocation log shows the mirror deliver when enabled (`messageId=0` synthetic inbound + `outbound send ok chatId=…`), then `[outbound/echo] Echo delivery suppressed … (destination disabled/revoked)` after the group is disabled. - **Observed result after fix**: (a) Enabled group: the pinned Telegram thread receives the webchat-triggered turn's real reply, rendered natively (verbose `💬`+`🛠️` per the destination's config), exactly once. (b) **Disabled (revoked) group**: zero sends to the group and the tdlib driver observing it reports `NO_NEW_MESSAGE` — neither the native mirror nor the echo fallback delivers. Confirmed on both hot-reload and gateway-restart disable, at current head `0193a3d`. - **What was not tested**: Nothing material remains unproven on current head. **Destination revocation is now proven live** (and a real gap it surfaced is fixed — see *Destination revocation* below). Multi-account routing is also proven on current head — see below. #### Multi-account routing — current head (account-aware dispatch) Two real Telegram accounts in one gateway: `default` = bot #2 in GID `-1003900553563`, `acct2` = bot #3 (`@jeeves_oc_acct2_bot`) in a separate forum group GID2 `-1004389798714` (bot #3 promoted admin; bot #2 is **not** a member of GID2). The echo target for GID2 is set with `accountId: acct2`, then a **webchat-origin** turn drives the session. Gateway debug log (`.proof/pin-from-here-multiaccount-log.txt`): ```text [telegram] [acct2] starting provider (@jeeves_oc_acct2_bot) [telegram] [default] starting provider (@JeeevesOpenClawTelegram2bot) message received: channel=webchat ... sessionKey=agent:main:telegram:group:-1004389798714:topic:1 message dispatch started: channel=telegram ... source=replyResolver ← the mirror message dispatch completed: channel=telegram ... source=replyResolver outcome=completed ``` The webchat-origin turn mirrored into GID2 (`source=replyResolver` = the bus-sourced mirror), with **zero** `mirror: no dispatcher` fail-closed warnings (the acct2 dispatcher resolved). Because **bot #2 is not in GID2** (`getChatMember` → `chat not found`), a successful mirror delivery there could *only* be the **acct2** account (bot #3) — proving the mirror routes through the target's own account, not the first-registered/default one. #### Destination revocation — live proof (and a gap it caught, now fixed) Proving this **live** (real Telegram `/pin` via the tdlib user-driver, not unit-only) surfaced a real gap the unit tests missed: the revocation gate only covered the **native mirror** (which re-runs the channel's inbound admission). The prompt / post-hoc **echo** path (`fireEchoDeliveries`) delivers through the *channel-agnostic* raw send, which has **no** admission gate — so a disabled pinned group kept receiving echoes, and when the mirror fan-out didn't fire (e.g. right after the channel restart that disabling a group triggers) the post-hoc echo leaked the conversation to the revoked destination. **Fix** (commit `083338a`): a per-channel **echo-admission predicate** (`channel-admission.ts`), registered per account exactly like the mirror dispatcher (last-wins, unregister on stop, **fail closed**). `fireEchoDeliveries` filters targets through it; telegram registers a predicate that reports group/topic-disabled from its live config. Now a disabled destination gets **neither** the native mirror **nor** the echo fallback. **Stop/reload window** (commit `8519433`): a channel that unregisters its admission predicate (account stop/reload drops the predicate *and* the dispatcher together) must not revert to admit-all. A sticky, import-cycle-free `channel-mirror-capability` marker records channels that have registered a mirror dispatcher; the echo gate then **fails closed** for a mirror-capable channel whenever no predicate resolves, so the raw echo can't leak to a revoked destination during the unregister window. Non-mirror channels admit echoes unchanged. Unit-covered (`channel-admission.test.ts`). **Telegram DM denial** (commits `219810d`, `fbd5ee2`): the same gap existed for a pinned **DM**. Three parts: (a) `enforceTelegramDmAccess` denial returned null *without* the revocation marker → `dispatchMirror` threw → unmark → raw echo could reach the denied DM; now a denied DM in a mirror context calls `onMirrorAdmissionBlocked` (keep the mark, suppress the post-hoc). (b) The synthetic mirror inbound used sender id `0`, so the DM access gate *rejected every already-authorized (paired/allowlisted) DM*; for a private chat the chat id **is** the target user's id, so it's now the synthetic sender — an authorized DM mirrors, a revoked one is denied. (c) The echo-admission predicate now re-runs **live DM authorization** (`resolveTelegramDmAllow` + `isTelegramDmAccessAllowed`) for a direct chat, so the raw echo also honors DM policy/pairing/allowlist revocation (the predicate is async; admission errors fail closed). Unit-covered: `bot-message-context.mirror.test.ts` (DM-denial → `onMirrorAdmissionBlocked`), `channel-admission.test.ts` (async predicate + fail-closed), `echo.test.ts` (async suppression). **Live-proven** (real DM: driver/Cameron DMs bot #2, `/pin on` in the DM): with `dmPolicy: open` the authorized DM mirrors (validating the sender-identity fix); after `dmPolicy: disabled` the DM gets **zero** sends, the driver observing it reports `NO_NEW_MESSAGE`, and the gateway logs `Echo delivery suppressed … telegram:8661849123 (destination disabled/revoked)` — see the *DIRECT-DM REVOCATION* section of [`.proof/pin-from-here-revocation.txt`](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anagnorisis2peripeteia/openclaw/proof/pin-from-here-evidence/.proof/pin-from-here-revocation.txt). **Registry ownership** (open maintainer decision): the mirror-dispatcher + echo-admission registries are global, caller-keyed, last-wins maps with no per-plugin ownership enforcement. They are exported from `openclaw/plugin-sdk/channel-outbound` and **only the bundled telegram channel uses them**. Whether to keep them as a public seam, hand them out through an owner-scoped registrar, or keep them internal is a maintainer API call — flagged explicitly in `docs/plugins/sdk-overview.md`. (An attempt to hide them on a repo-local subpath was reverted: it fought the extension package-boundary + surface-budget guards for no real gain.) The branch is also **merged up to current `openclaw/openclaw` main** (conflicts in `send.ts` / `agent-runner-execution.ts` resolved). The plugin-SDK surface-budget guard is kept and passing; its budgets were raised to the merged-main surface actuals (the +10 entrypoints / +333 exports growth is from main's 203 commits, not this PR's ~6 exports). **Live proof** (single gateway; control then revocation; the group is a real forum supergroup, pinned via a genuine `/pin on` Telegram command): ```text # CONTROL (group ENABLED): the mirror delivers message received: channel=telegram messageId=0 ... source=dispatchInboundMessage ← native mirror [telegram] outbound send ok ... chatId=-1003900553563 messageId=404 ← lands in the group # disable the group, then drive again: [reload] config hot reload applied (channels.telegram.groups.-1003900553563.enabled) [outbound/echo] Echo delivery suppressed for telegram:telegram:-1003900553563 (destination disabled/revoked) ``` - **Before the fix:** the driver observed `📱 [via webchat] …` arriving in the disabled group (+1 send). - **After the fix:** `sends-to-GID after revoke = control count (0 added)`, and the **tdlib driver observing the group reports `NO_NEW_MESSAGE`** — the disabled destination receives nothing. Confirmed on **both** revocation paths: config hot-reload **and** a full gateway restart. Unit coverage: `channel-admission.test.ts` (registry + fail-closed) and an `echo.test.ts` suppression case. Full redacted run + gateway log for both paths: [`.proof/pin-from-here-revocation.txt`](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anagnorisis2peripeteia/openclaw/proof/pin-from-here-evidence/.proof/pin-from-here-revocation.txt). The same run filmed in Telegram Desktop — the bot rendering into GID2 is **"Jeeves OC Test Account 2" (`@jeeves_oc_acct2_bot` = acct2)**, mirroring the webchat-origin turn (`📱 [via webchat] …`) and its reply natively: Account-aware dispatch also fails closed on an **explicit account mismatch** (a target pinning a different account than the only registered one is never mirrored through the wrong account) — unit-regressed. Recreation of the Mantis telegram-desktop flow on a local patched crabbox: a real gateway built from this branch (current `openclaw/openclaw` main + this work), Telegram Desktop + the user-driver + screen capture, driven by a **real `claude-cli/claude-sonnet-4-6` backend with an exec tool call** (not a mock). A Telegram thread runs `/pin on`; webchat then drives the same session with a tool-using prompt (`sleep 3; hostname` — output not derivable from context). The pinned thread renders the **webchat-origin turn's reply natively** — including the real tool result — through telegram's own dispatch: 1. `/verbose on` → "⚙️ Verbose logging enabled." 2. `/pin on` → **"📌 Pinned. This thread will mirror turns from other threads of this session."** 3. `📱 [via webchat] Use your exec (bash) tool to run … sleep 3; hostname …` 4. The pinned thread receives the real reply: **"The hostname is `crabbox-golden-crayfish-fa445ed1`."** — the actual box hostname, i.e. a real `hostname` exec run by the webchat-triggered turn, rendered natively in the pinned Telegram thread via the channel-agnostic mirror dispatch. Mechanism confirmed in the gateway log for the filmed run: `launchMirrorDispatch` fires for the webchat-origin turn (origin = webchat, telegram target not self-excluded, dispatcher found); the telegram `dispatchMirror` builds the synthetic inbound (`context=BUILT`) and runs `dispatchTelegramMessage` for the full turn (`message processed: channel=telegram messageId=0 … completed`); exactly one reply lands (post-hoc assistant echo skipped by the handled-mark, no duplicate). #### Riding the destination's verbose config Because the mirror re-runs each target's own dispatch, it inherits *that* account's streaming/verbose config rather than forcing anything. With the daily driver's setup (`verboseDefault: on`, `streaming.mode: progress`, reasoning + `toolProgressDetail: raw`), a webchat-triggered turn renders the **full interleaved durable record** in the pinned thread — assistant commentary (`💬`) and tool summaries (`🛠️`) as standalone messages, exactly as a native turn there would (matching #91976): This exercised a real gap: the CLI runner emits `stream:"tool"` events with both `phase:"start"` and `phase:"result"`, and the resolver was routing every tool event to `onToolStart` — so the durable `🛠️` summaries (driven by `onToolResult`) were dropped from the mirror while `💬` commentary still rendered. Fixed by running the bus tool events through the same `createCliToolSummaryTracker` the native CLI dispatch uses, so the mirror's tool summaries are byte-identical, in both streaming modes; the target dispatch still gates the actual send on verbose. ### Public SDK contract The mirror-dispatcher seam (`registerChannelMirrorDispatcher` / `unregisterChannelMirrorDispatcher` / `MirrorDispatcher`) is now **documented** as a public `channel-outbound` contract in `docs/plugins/sdk-overview.md` (new *Channel mirror dispatcher* section): per-account registration, the last-wins reload lifecycle, and the account-match + destination-revocation behavior a channel plugin must honor (the native dispatcher is the sole delivery authority, so it must run the channel's own admission/revocation path). The barrel export points at that section. It stays on its neutral SDK subpath per the repo's internal-module convention rather than being reached into as an internal — final acceptance of the public surface is the maintainers' call. ### Tests + CI - `tsgo:core`: clean. Full `oxlint` shard gate (`run-oxlint-shards`): clean across the PR surface (type-aware rules included). - The PR-relevant vitest matrix is green (`infra`, `auto-reply-*`, `gateway-core`/ `methods`, `commands`, `extension-telegram`, `hooks`, `plugin-sdk`): the bus-sourced resolver (incl. the tool-summary replay), handled-mark dedup, and `/pin` fan-out are covered. The `gateway-server` shard's auth/device-identity failures are pre-existing and environmental — identical `15 failed | 92 passed` on pristine `main` (merge-base `9827490`) in the same container. ### AI assistance Built with AI assistance (Claude). The channel-agnostic design, implementation, tests, and the local telegram-desktop proof were produced with AI; the architecture direction (mirror through each channel's own dispatch, ride its config) and review are the author's.

target.title: feat(docs): replace-image command (replaceImage) — swap image bytes in place without losing position/size
target.body:
The Docs API `replaceImage` request swaps the bytes behind an existing inline image while preserving its position, size, and surrounding paragraphs — only the pixels change. `gog docs` has no command for it. Today the only way to refresh a diagram is `delete the positioned object + insert-image` again, which loses the original placement and re-applies a default width, so every refresh needs manual re-positioning.

This is a common workflow: a doc embeds a generated diagram, the source regenerates, and you want to drop in new bytes without disturbing layout.

Proposed:

```
gog docs replace-image <docId> --file <local.png>          # upload, then ReplaceImageRequest
gog docs replace-image <docId> --url <https-url>
  --match-alt <substring>   # pick when the doc has multiple inline images (match on alt/description)
  --object-id <id>          # explicit positioned-object id (from `gog docs images list`)
  --tab <tab>
```

`images list` already enumerates the inline objects, so an `--object-id` selector falls out naturally; `--match-alt` is the ergonomic path. The upload half can reuse whatever `insert-image --file` already does for restricted-tenant embedding.

Ref: documents.batchUpdate `ReplaceImageRequest` (imageObjectId, uri, imageReplaceMethod).

target.title: fix(whatsapp): add listGroupsLive for real-time group directory
target.body:
## Root Cause

The WhatsApp channel directory adapter only registered `listGroups` (which reads from the config map `channels.whatsapp.groups`) but did **not** register `listGroupsLive`. The CLI (and other consumers) prefer `listGroupsLive` when available, so users who have active WhatsApp group memberships but an empty config-based groups map would see an empty directory. No `listGroupsLive` implementation existed for WhatsApp.

## Summary

- Bug: WhatsApp directory `/groups` returned empty even when the account is a member of groups, because only the config-backed `listGroups` was wired up but not the live-fetch variant.
- This PR adds `listWhatsAppDirectoryGroupsLive` that fetches groups from the Baileys socket via `groupFetchAllParticipating()`, with graceful fallback to config when the socket is unavailable or throws. The new function is registered in the channel directory adapter.
- Fixes #76722

## Revision (2026-06-20)

- Fixed a P2 issue where live groups were filtered by JID only, so `--query "Family"` would not match a group named "Family Chat". Now builds entries first and filters by both JID and subject name (matching Discord/Slack live directory behavior).
- Commit: `e7ac40ee0e`

## Verification

- `pnpm test extensions/whatsapp/src/directory-config.test.ts` — 8/8 passed (6 new tests + 2 existing; +1 test for name-based filtering)
- `pnpm tsgo:test` — passed (type check)
- `pnpm lint:extensions:bundled` — passed
- Autoreview: clean, no accepted/actionable findings
- CI: The `checks-node-core-tooling` failure is an infrastructure/flaky issue unrelated to this PR (only touches `extensions/whatsapp/`)

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: WhatsApp directory `/groups` now returns the account's real group memberships fetched live from the WhatsApp connection, filtered by both JID and group name.
Real environment tested: Linux, Node 22, branch `fix/issue-76722-whatsapp-directory-groups-live` commit `e7ac40ee0e`
Exact steps or command run after this patch:
```bash
pnpm test extensions/whatsapp/src/directory-config.test.ts
pnpm tsgo:test
pnpm lint:extensions:bundled
```
Evidence after fix:
```console
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/whatsapp/src/directory-config.test.ts

 RUN  v4.1.8

 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  8 passed (8)
   Start at  00:50:52
   Duration  25.63s
```
Observed result after fix: All 8 tests pass including the new test for name-based query filtering. Type check and lint pass cleanly.
What was not tested: Live E2E with a real WhatsApp account (requires Crabbox with WhatsApp auth); no WhatsApp directory binary/E2E test infrastructure exists.

target.title: perf: split subagent registry runtime imports

## Summary

- Split the subagent registry lazy runtime imports across three narrower runtime entries: `context-engine-init.runtime`, `context-engine-registry.runtime`, and `runtime-plugins.runtime`.

- Add source-side wrapper entries so source-mode tests and built `dist` artifacts resolve the same relative runtime specifiers.

## Real behavior proof

target.title: fix(active-memory): exclude dreaming-narrative session keys from interactive eligibility gate
target.body:
## Summary

- AM eligibility gate (`isEligibleInteractiveSession`) accepted dreaming-narrative cron session keys because they share `trigger="user"` with real conversational sessions.
- Each false-accepted invocation paid the full active-memory cold-start cost inside a hardcoded 45000ms command-lane budget, reliably causing timeouts during cron windows.
- Added a guard clause that returns `false` when the session key matches canonical dreaming-narrative patterns (bare or agent-prefixed `dreaming-narrative-{light|rem|deep}-...`). Uses a narrow regex instead of a broad substring match to avoid false positives on real chat session IDs that happen to contain the phrase.
- Fixes #78500

## Verification

- `pnpm test extensions/active-memory` — 150 tests passed
- P2 review finding (broad substring match) addressed: narrowed to canonical session-key regex, added regression test for non-canonical substring
- Autoreview: clean

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed:** active-memory eligibility gate was accepting dreaming-narrative cron session keys as "interactive", causing cold-start timeouts in the 45s lane budget during cron windows. Also narrowed the exclusion from a broad substring match to canonical session-key patterns.

**Real environment tested:** Linux 4.19.112, Node 22.19, branch fix/active-memory-dreaming-gate at 99ad2a2383

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:**
```bash
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/active-memory/index.test.ts \
  -t "does not run for dreaming|allows non-canonical session" \
  --reporter verbose
```

**Evidence after fix (terminal output from runtime hook invocation):**
```console
 ✓ |extension-active-memory| extensions/active-memory/index.test.ts > active-memory plugin > does not run for dreaming-narrative cron session keys 85ms
 ✓ |extension-active-memory| extensions/active-memory/index.test.ts > active-memory plugin > allows non-canonical session keys that merely contain the dreaming-narrative substring 710ms

 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  2 passed | 148 skipped (150)
```

**Observed result after fix:** Both behavioral tests pass:
1. Canonical dreaming-narrative session keys (`agent:main:dreaming-narrative-light-abc123`) are correctly excluded — `before_prompt_build` returns `undefined` and `runEmbeddedAgent` is not called.
2. Non-canonical session keys that merely contain the substring (`agent:main:webchat:dreaming-narrative-room`, a real chat topic) remain eligible — `runEmbeddedAgent` is called and the hook proceeds normally.

**What was not tested:** Live cron window reproduction with actual dreaming-narrative scheduler (requires full runtime integration test); the eligibility gate behavior is verified at the `before_prompt_build` hook level where the decision is made.

target.title: fix(agents): rename timezone-only prompt section (Fixes #63181)
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #63181.

Rename the timezone-only system prompt section from `Current Date & Time` to `Time Zone`.

This keeps the prompt wording aligned with current behavior: the section only injects the configured timezone to preserve cache stability, and agents should still use `session_status` when they need the live date/time.

Scope stays narrow:

- rename the emitted system prompt heading
- update the adjacent prompt test
- update the directly related docs that describe this section

Non-goals:

- no change to timestamp injection behavior
- no change to `session_status`
- no reintroduction of dynamic date/time into the system prompt

## Tests and validation

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/system-prompt.test.ts`
- `git diff --check`

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed:

- the live system prompt should no longer present a timezone-only section under the misleading `Current Date & Time` heading

Local proof attempted:

- direct prompt-builder invocation from this clean worktree via `tsx` to print the rendered section

Observed result:

- the focused system prompt test passed after the heading rename
- a standalone `tsx` eval path in this worktree failed before prompt rendering because local workspace package resolution for `@openclaw/fs-safe/config` is not available in this environment

What was not tested:

- a fully booted runtime render of the prompt in a running OpenClaw session

Why this is still meaningful:

- the changed runtime surface is a single string emitted by `buildTimeSection()` in `src/agents/system-prompt.ts`
- the adjacent test exercises the production prompt builder directly and verifies the rendered heading and timezone line together

## Risk

Low risk.

This is a wording-only fix to better match the existing cache-stable timezone behavior. It does not change prompt timing semantics, model routing, tool access, or any runtime date/time source.

target.title: fix(plugin-runtime): restore channel turn alias
target.body:
## Summary

- Restores the deprecated `core.channel.turn.*` runtime surface as an alias of `core.channel.inbound.*` for shipped channel plugins compiled before the inbound rename.
- Keeps the alias as the same object as `inbound`, so there is no second implementation path or drift in production runtime behavior.
- Updates the PluginRuntime type, plugin runtime mock, API baseline hash, and SDK docs to describe the compatibility alias while keeping new plugin guidance on `inbound.*`.
- Intentionally does not change bundled Feishu source, channel logic, config, or package artifacts.

## Linked context

Closes #91122

Related #91122

Was this requested by a maintainer or owner? No direct maintainer request; this is a focused compatibility fix for the reported shipped plugin runtime break.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

Behavior addressed: Restores `runtime.channel.turn.*` / `core.channel.turn.*` as a deprecated compatibility alias for old channel plugins while preserving `runtime.channel.inbound.*` as the canonical API.

Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw source checkout on macOS, Node 24-compatible repo toolchain, branch `fix/91122-runtime-channel-turn-alias` after this patch.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: Built the runtime, then ran this Node probe against the generated `dist/runtime-channel-UUbgZd8n.js` chunk:

```console
$ node --input-type=module - <<'NODE'
import { createRuntimeChannel } from './dist/runtime-channel-UUbgZd8n.js';
const channel = createRuntimeChannel({
  providerControl: {},
  createUnifiedProviderSetup: () => ({}),
  ensureRuntimeAuthDeps: () => ({}),
  runtimeAdapters: {},
  createAgentStateDB: () => ({}),
  createAgentStateDBForStorePath: () => ({}),
  registerInboundSession: () => {},
  runWithDispatch: () => {},
});
console.log(JSON.stringify({
  hasTurn: Boolean(channel.turn),
  sameObject: channel.turn === channel.inbound,
  sameRun: channel.turn.run === channel.inbound.run,
  sameBuildContext: channel.turn.buildContext === channel.inbound.buildContext,
  sameDispatchReply: channel.turn.dispatchReply === channel.inbound.dispatchReply,
  sameRunPreparedReply: channel.turn.runPreparedReply === channel.inbound.runPreparedReply,
}));
NODE
```

Evidence after fix:

```console
{"hasTurn":true,"sameObject":true,"sameRun":true,"sameBuildContext":true,"sameDispatchReply":true,"sameRunPreparedReply":true}
```

Observed result after fix: The built runtime exposes `channel.turn`; `channel.turn === channel.inbound`; all four legacy functions are the same function references as the canonical inbound runtime helpers.

What was not tested: I did not install `openclaw@2026.6.x` with `@openclaw/feishu@2026.5.28` or run a live Feishu DM against real credentials.

Proof limitations or environment constraints: The proof validates the exact built runtime compatibility surface that old compiled plugins dereference. It does not prove a full Feishu WebSocket setup because that requires live Feishu credentials and an older published plugin install.

Before evidence (optional but encouraged): Issue #91122 reports old plugins failing on `core.channel.turn.run()` because the runtime only exposed `channel.inbound.*`.

## Tests and validation

Commands run:

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/plugins/runtime/runtime-channel.test.ts src/plugin-sdk/test-helpers/plugin-runtime-mock.test.ts`
- `OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 OPENCLAW_VITEST_FS_MODULE_CACHE_PATH=.artifacts/vitest-cache-91122 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/plugin-sdk/test-helpers/plugin-runtime-mock.test.ts`

- `pnpm plugin-sdk:api:gen`
- `pnpm plugin-sdk:api:check`
- `pnpm build:plugin-sdk:strict-smoke`
- `pnpm docs:check-mdx`
- `pnpm format:docs:check`
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/plugins/runtime/types-channel.ts src/plugins/runtime/runtime-channel.ts src/plugins/runtime/runtime-channel.test.ts src/plugin-sdk/test-helpers/plugin-runtime-mock.ts src/plugin-sdk/test-helpers/plugin-runtime-mock.test.ts`
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/plugin-sdk/test-helpers/plugin-runtime-mock.test.ts`
- `git diff --check`
- `node scripts/ui.js build`
- `.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode local`

Regression coverage added or updated:

- `src/plugins/runtime/runtime-channel.test.ts` now asserts deprecated `channel.turn` is wired to `channel.inbound` with object/function identity.
- `src/plugin-sdk/test-helpers/plugin-runtime-mock.test.ts` now asserts the test helper exposes the same alias and that inbound overrides are visible through `turn`.

What failed before this fix, if known:

- Old compiled plugins calling `core.channel.turn.run()` could dereference `undefined.run` after the runtime rename removed the `turn` key.

If no test was added, why not?

Notes:

I ran the final UI build stage separately with `node scripts/ui.js build`, which passed. An earlier full `pnpm build` completed successfully on this worktree before the final test-helper alias correction.

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? (`Yes/No`) Yes, old shipped channel plugins can call the deprecated runtime alias again.

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (`Yes/No`) No.

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (`Yes/No`) No.

target.title: fix(ios): reset sidebar navigation stacks

## Summary

- Fixes the iPad/sidebar navigation regression where a pushed Settings detail could survive after selecting another sidebar root such as Overview, Chat, or Talk.
- Makes embedded sidebar/phone detail views use their owning shell `NavigationStack` instead of creating competing root stacks.
- Keeps the iPhone Control hub safe by clearing its local detail path before handing off to root tabs.
- Keeps the iPad drawer sidebar interactive after a pushed Settings route by moving the dimming tap layer outside the sidebar hit area.
- Out of scope: broader iOS layout redesign, settings content changes, or unrelated stale source guard expectations.

Review focus: `RootTabs` shell ownership, embedded `ownsNavigationStack` usage, drawer hit-testing, and the regression guards in the RootTabs tests.

## Linked context

Closes: none

Related: none

Requested by maintainer during iOS 27 navigation crash/bounce investigation.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: iPad sidebar/drawer navigation could stay stuck on a pushed Settings subpage after selecting Overview; same navigation-shell cleanup was applied to iPhone Control handoff paths.

- What was not tested: physical iOS 27 install/connect/log proof is now captured; the original Overview bounce/crash tap path is still covered by simulator before/after GIFs rather than a physical-device video.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: local `origin/main` required the repo-standard `pnpm ios:gen` project regeneration before XcodeBuildMCP testing because the generated Xcode project had stale WatchExtension paths after the watch app move.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged):

Before fix, the same interaction remains on Channels / Integrations after tapping Overview:

![Before: Settings subpage remains stale after Overview tap](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/Solvely-Colin/pr-proof-assets/main/openclaw-ios-navigation-stack-reset/before-ipad-settings-overview.gif)

Raw before recording: https://raw.githubusercontent.com/Solvely-Colin/pr-proof-assets/main/openclaw-ios-navigation-stack-reset/before-ipad-settings-overview.mp4

## Tests and validation

Commands/proof run:

- `pnpm ios:gen`
- XcodeBuildMCP iPad focused tests: `-only-testing:OpenClawTests/RootTabsSidebarRegressionTests -only-testing:OpenClawTests/RootTabsPresentationTests -only-testing:OpenClawTests/SwiftUIRenderSmokeTests` -> 57 passed, 0 failed.
- XcodeBuildMCP iPhone focused tests: same focused suite -> 57 passed, 0 failed.
- `.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode branch --base origin/main` -> clean, no accepted/actionable findings.

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

Yes. Sidebar and phone Control navigation now clear stale detail paths instead of preserving pushed subpages across root switches.

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

No.

What is the highest-risk area?

How is that risk mitigated?

The default standalone behavior still owns local stacks; only embedded shell contexts opt out. Focused simulator tests and before/after recordings cover both the failing iPad path and the iPhone safety path.

target.title: fix(tui): show last tool-error summary on aborted runs
target.body:
## Summary

When a run ends while looping on tool-call validation errors, the TUI previously
showed only `run aborted`, making a validation-failure loop indistinguishable from
a model crash, a network timeout, or a context-overflow abort. This surfaces the
last tool failure as a sanitized one-line summary on the abort line, so the user can
recognize a schema-mismatch loop and intervene.

Closes #90982

## Problem

`validateToolArguments` already throws a detailed error, the agent loop already
catches it into an error tool result, and `lastToolError` is already tracked. The
information exists in the pipeline — the TUI abort branch simply never read it and
rendered a fixed string. This is a wiring gap, not a missing mechanism.

## Fix

Reuses the existing `lastToolError` / `ToolErrorSummary` plumbing and lifecycle
metadata; no new mechanism:

- `handleAgentEnd` (lifecycle handler) summarizes `lastToolError` onto the terminal
  lifecycle metadata as `toolErrorSummary`, **gated to error/abort terminals** so
  clean ends stay metadata-free.
- `EmbeddedTuiBackend` reads `toolErrorSummary` off the lifecycle event and passes it
  as the chat `errorMessage` on aborted chat events.
- The TUI abort branch renders `run aborted: <message>` when present, otherwise the
  unchanged `run aborted`.

**Sanitization:** the summary is cut at the `Received arguments:` marker that
`validation.ts` appends, so the JSON echo of the model's arguments never reaches the
terminal or the lifecycle event.

## Out of scope (deliberately)

- **No retry-policy / loop-abort changes.** There is no "abort after N validation
  failures" guard today — runs in this state are terminated by the idle/run timeout.
  This PR only surfaces the last tool failure that preceded that termination.
- No tool-call parser or model changes.
- No new config option.

## Why no repeat count

The abort line omits an `N×` count by design. A run only aborts *because* it looped,
so seeing `edit tool validation failed: ...` on an abort already implies repetition.
The actionable signal is the category (which tool, which schema error), not the exact
retry count — and there is no place in the pipeline that naturally produces a count
without adding new state to a hot path. A repeat counter belongs with a future
early-abort feature, not with this visibility fix.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

* **Behavior or issue addressed:** the TUI rendered a bare `run aborted` for every
  aborted run, hiding tool-call validation-loop failures (#90982). This patch surfaces
  the last tool failure as a one-line, argument-free summary on the abort line.
* **Real environment tested:** shipped `openclaw@2026.6.1` from npm
  (`dist/tui-C77l8t12.js`) for BEFORE; this branch's real source for AFTER.
  Node v22.22.1, Windows 10 (19045 / 22H2), win32 x64.
* **Exact steps or command run after this patch:**
  1. `npm pack openclaw@2026.6.1` and extracted the shipped build. Its abort render in
     `dist/tui-C77l8t12.js` is `addSystem("run aborted")` (unconditional), and the build
     contains neither `summarizeToolErrorForAbort` nor a `toolErrorSummary` field (grep: 0 / 0).
  2. Ran this branch's real `summarizeToolErrorForAbort` (imported from the actual
     `src/agents/tool-error-summary.ts`) against the exact string `validateToolArguments`
     builds (`packages/llm-core/src/validation.ts:320-328`, bullet prefix `  - ` at :323) for
     the reporter's malformed root-level `{path, oldText, newText}` edit call, then applied
     the patched abort-render formula verbatim from `src/tui/tui-event-handlers.ts:682`
     (`evt.errorMessage ? \`run aborted: ${evt.errorMessage}\` : "run aborted"`).
* **Evidence after fix (console output):**
BEFORE — shipped openclaw@2026.6.1 (dist/tui-C77l8t12.js: addSystem("run aborted"))
"run aborted"
AFTER — reporter's real case (2 issues)
"run aborted: edit tool validation failed: edits: must have required properties edits; root: must not have additional properties"
leak check: no (ok)
AFTER — single-issue (matches the PR description example)
"run aborted: edit tool validation failed: edits: must have required properties edits"
leak check: no (ok)

* **Observed result after fix:** the abort line now names the failing tool and the schema
  error, so a validation-failure loop is distinguishable from a crash / timeout / context
  abort. Multiple issues join on one line; a single issue matches the PR description verbatim.
  A planted secret (`sk-PLANTED-SECRET-...`) in the edit args never appears on the line, and
  neither does `Received arguments:` — confirming the argument payload is stripped.
* **Production chain verified end-to-end (not two isolated halves):**
  `validateToolArguments` throws → stored as `lastToolError.error` (`validation.ts:326`) →
  lifecycle handler computes `summarizeToolErrorForAbort(lastToolError)` →
  `terminalMeta.toolErrorSummary` (`embedded-agent-subscribe.handlers.lifecycle.ts:143`) →
  `emitChatAborted(runId, run, toolErrorSummary)` (`embedded-backend.ts:932`) →
  emits `{ state:"aborted", errorMessage }` (`embedded-backend.ts:825`) → TUI renders the
  formula above (`tui-event-handlers.ts:682`). The transport between only forwards the string.
* **What was not tested:** a full live TUI session driving the reporter's reasoning-MoE
  models (Nemotron-3-Super, gpt-oss-120b) under vLLM to the idle/run timeout, i.e. the live
  TTY paint + inter-process transport segment. The summarizer is pure and was exercised
  directly on its real source; the abort-render path is identical whether the aborted
  terminal comes from the timeout or a manual stop.
* **Before evidence:** see the BEFORE block above, taken from the unpatched shipped
  `2026.6.1` build (`addSystem("run aborted")`).
  
## Reproduction note

A live repro of the original loop requires the reporter's specific reasoning-MoE
models (Nemotron-3-Super, gpt-oss-120b) plus the vLLM stack, which isn't practical to
stand up here. Instead the render path is covered by deterministic tests that simulate
an aborted run after a failed tool result — the approach the review itself noted as
valid ("A focused test can simulate an aborted run after a failed tool result").

## Testing

- `src/agents/tool-error-summary.test.ts` (new) — the summarizer: single/multiple
  validation errors, non-validation errors, empty input, truncation, and a **leak
  test** asserting a planted secret in the echoed args never appears in the output.
- `src/agents/embedded-agent-subscribe.handlers.lifecycle.test.ts` — aborted terminals
  carry `toolErrorSummary`; clean ends stay metadata-free; a second-layer leak
  assertion that `Received arguments:` never rides the lifecycle event.
- `src/tui/embedded-backend.test.ts` — the lifecycle `toolErrorSummary` is carried onto
  the aborted chat event as `errorMessage`.
- `src/tui/tui-event-handlers.test.ts` — the abort line includes the summary when
  present and falls back to bare `run aborted` when absent.

The summary routes through the lifecycle handler, so coverage lives in
`lifecycle.test.ts`; the originally-suggested `payloads.errors.test.ts` is unchanged
and still passes.

Local run: **206 passed** (`node scripts/run-vitest.mjs`); `oxfmt --check` clean,
`oxlint` exit 0, `tsgo:core` and `tsgo:test` exit 0.

target.title: fix(agents): inherit default agent model catalog for secondary agents
target.body:
# fix(agents): inherit default agent model catalog for secondary agents

## Summary

Secondary (non-default) agents created with `openclaw agents add` fail at runtime
with `FailoverError: Unknown model: google/gemini-2.5-flash` when using a
Google/Gemini model authenticated via `GEMINI_API_KEY`. The default/`main` agent
works with the same model and key.

**Root cause:** model discovery only runs for the default agent at gateway
startup, so generated plugin model catalogs (`plugins/<provider>/catalog.json`)
are only populated under the default agent dir. A secondary agent gets an
apiKey-only google catalog with **no models**. `ModelRegistry.loadCustomModels()`
loads plugin catalogs only from the current agent dir, with no read-through to
the default agent — unlike auth profiles, which already inherit via
`inheritedAuthDir`.

## The fix

Add read-through inheritance for generated plugin model catalogs, mirroring the
existing auth-profile read-through (`inheritedAuthDir`). Implementation is
**local-first with a per-provider gap-fill gate**:

- `ModelRegistry` accepts an optional `inheritedAgentDir`. The agent's OWN
  catalogs (root models.json + local plugin catalogs) load first and establish
  its full local state (models + provider request config + per-model headers).
- Inheritance is **gated per provider**: a provider the local agent already has
  any model for is excluded from the inherited load entirely — it contributes no
  provider request config, no per-model headers, and no models. So "local always
  wins" never depends on write order, and the inherited catalog can neither read
  nor overwrite the local agent's request config/headers (closes the
  cross-agent boundary concern). Provider request config (baseUrl/api/auth/
  headers) is per-provider with a single slot, so this also avoids pairing an
  inherited model with a local provider's credential.
- Only providers the secondary agent does not configure at all are inherited
  (config + headers + models) — exactly the agent living on inheritance. `apiKey`
  values in catalogs are env refs (e.g. `GEMINI_API_KEY`) resolved per-agent, so
  no secret material crosses the agent boundary.
- A broken/invalid inherited catalog is skipped, never breaking the local agent.
- `discoverModels` threads `inheritedAgentDir` through to the registry.
- `discoverCachedAgentStores` passes the resolved default agent dir, and the
  inherited catalog files are folded into the discovery-cache fingerprint so
  changes to the default agent's catalog invalidate the secondary agent's cached
  snapshot.

The default agent never inherits from itself (path-equality guard), so its
behavior is unchanged.

A heavier alternative — running model discovery for every agent at startup — is
the deeper root-cause fix but is costly (per-agent network discovery, needs to
be lazy) and much larger in scope; noting it as a possible follow-up.

## Files

- `src/agents/sessions/model-registry.ts` — `inheritedAgentDir` option + read-through merge
- `src/agents/agent-model-discovery.ts` — thread option through `discoverModels`
- `src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/model-discovery-cache.ts` — pass default dir + fingerprint
- `src/agents/sessions/model-registry.test.ts` — 6 new tests

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: A secondary agent created with `openclaw agents add`
  could not use `google/gemini-2.5-flash` (authed via `GEMINI_API_KEY`) and failed
  every turn with `Unknown model: google/gemini-2.5-flash`, while the default
  `main` agent worked with the same model and key.
- Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw 2026.6.2 install on macOS (launchd
  gateway), Google Gemini via `GEMINI_API_KEY`, default model
  `google/gemini-2.5-flash`, with a real `main` agent plus a secondary agent
  (`english-tutor`).
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: Applied the patch to my install,
  ran `pnpm build`, restarted the gateway, emptied the secondary agent's google
  plugin catalog to the apiKey-only state (the state `agents add` leaves behind),
  then ran a turn through the running gateway — the same lane a Telegram DM uses:
  `openclaw agent --agent english-tutor -m "Hi Lexi, correct this: I has two cats yesterday." --json`
- Evidence after fix (copied live output from the running gateway):
  ```
  $ cat ~/.openclaw/agents/english-tutor/agent/plugins/google/catalog.json
  {"generatedBy":"openclaw-plugin-model-catalog-v1","providers":{"google":{"apiKey":"GEMINI_API_KEY"}}}   # no models

  $ openclaw agent --agent english-tutor -m "Hi Lexi, correct this: I has two cats yesterday." --json
  "status": "ok"
  "text": "...Câu đúng phải là: \"I **had** two cats yesterday.\" ... (real model reply)"
  ```
  And the patched discovery path exercised directly against the real on-disk agent
  dirs (`discoverModels`):
  ```
  secondary agent dir : ~/.openclaw/agents/english-tutor/agent   (catalog: apiKey-only, no models)
  BEFORE (no inheritance): Unknown model (undefined)
  AFTER  (inherit main)  : google/gemini-2.5-flash  api=google-generative-ai  baseUrl=https://generativelanguage.googleapis.com/v1beta
  ```
- Observed result after fix: The secondary agent resolved
  `google/gemini-2.5-flash` via read-through inheritance, through the native
  `google-generative-ai` transport, and produced a real model reply — with **no
  manual catalog seeding**. Before the patch the identical gateway turn returned
  `Unknown model: google/gemini-2.5-flash`. Re-verified end-to-end on the final
  build with the per-provider gating: a secondary agent that configures no
  provider of its own inherits the default agent's google catalog and resolves
  the model (a provider the secondary configures itself would instead be
  local-owned and not inherited).
- What was not tested: A second independent reproduction on a non-macOS host.
  Full `pnpm test` was run; 4 shards each reported 1 failing test, all unrelated
  to this change (macOS `/private/var` path-normalization / packaged-worker /
  fixture assertions; none of those files reference the changed modules).
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: Verified on a single macOS
  workstation. The live gateway turn was invoked via the CLI `agent` command
  (which routes through the running gateway on the same agent lane as a Telegram
  DM) rather than by sending an actual Telegram message.
- Before evidence (optional): `openclaw agent --agent <secondary> -m "..."` →
  `GatewayClientRequestError: FailoverError: Unknown model: google/gemini-2.5-flash`
  (default `main` agent returned `"status": "ok"` for the same model).

## Tests and validation

Supplemental (not a substitute for the real behavior proof above):

```
pnpm build        # exit 0
pnpm tsgo:all     # full prod + test typecheck, all projects — exit 0, 0 errors
npx oxlint <4 touched files>   # no warnings
npx vitest run src/agents/sessions/model-registry.test.ts src/agents/agent-model-discovery*.test.ts   # all pass
```
New tests cover: (1) a secondary agent inherits the default agent's catalog
models; (2) no inheritance without `inheritedAgentDir`; (3) the secondary agent's
own catalog wins over inherited models; (4)+(5) an inherited catalog never
overwrites a local provider's request config/headers and never leaks sibling
models into a provider the local agent owns — asserted at the **request
resolution** level (`getApiKeyAndHeaders` returns the local headers), for both the
**root models.json** and **local plugin catalog** paths; (6) an inherited-only
provider resolves with the inherited `baseUrl` and headers.

## CI status (rebased onto current `origin/main` `e5a9c60851`)

This branch is rebased onto `origin/main` HEAD (`e5a9c60851`). Most of the CI
failures present on the earlier base have since been fixed upstream. Current state:

| Check | CI result | Local on this branch |
| --- | --- | --- |
| `check-lint` | ✅ pass | `pnpm lint:core` exit 0 |
| `check-additional-runtime-topology-architecture` | ✅ pass | `pnpm check:architecture` — 0 cycles |
| `check-additional-extension-bundled` | ✅ pass | `pnpm lint:extensions:bundled` exit 0 |
| `build-artifacts` | ✅ pass | — |
| `check-test-types` | ❌ fail | reproduces identically on **clean `origin/main`** |

The single remaining `check-test-types` failure is **pre-existing on `main`** and
unrelated to this PR — it is two type errors in an extension test:

```
extensions/qa-lab/src/live-transports/whatsapp/cli.runtime.test.ts(48,5): error TS2322
extensions/qa-lab/src/live-transports/whatsapp/cli.runtime.test.ts(72,5): error TS2322
```

Verified by checking out clean `origin/main` (`e5a9c60851`) with none of this PR's
commits and running `pnpm tsgo:extensions:test` → the identical two errors, exit 2.
This PR changes only `src/agents/**` and does not touch `extensions/qa-lab/**`
(`git diff --name-only origin/main HEAD` lists four files, all under `src/agents/`),
so it cannot introduce this failure. The change's own surface is green: targeted
`vitest` for `model-registry`/`agent-model-discovery` passes and `tsgo` reports no
errors in the changed files.

## AI assistance

Drafted with Claude Code (Opus); reviewed and validated by me. Session log
available on request.

target.title: fix(tui): persist canonical provider in session modelProvider
target.body:
## Summary

After a CLI-routed agent run (any model with `agentRuntime.id` pinned to a CLI backend such as `claude-cli` or `google-antigravity-cli`), the TUI footer renders `claude-cli/claude-sonnet-4-6` instead of `anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-6` — leaking the implementation-routing id into the user-facing status line.

Root cause: `setSessionRuntimeModel(next, { provider: providerUsed, ... })` in `src/agents/command/session-store.ts` was called with the runtime id as `provider`, persisting it as `sessionInfo.modelProvider`. The footer renderer (`src/tui/tui.ts:~1183`) just reads that value.

Fix: canonicalize at the single setter call site using the existing `resolveCliRuntimeCanonicalProvider` helper (`src/agents/cli-backends.ts:245-271`). Only the persisted display value changes; the raw `providerUsed` continues to drive runtime-dependent logic (`isCliProvider`, CLI session bookkeeping).

## Scope

- `src/agents/command/session-store.ts` — call canonicalization helper before `setSessionRuntimeModel` in the non-preserve branch.
- `src/agents/command/session-store.test.ts` — vi.mock `../cli-backends.js` with the lookup convention used for `isCliProvider`; add failing-first test.

## Deliberately Out Of Scope

- The `preserveRuntimeModel` branch (lines 160-179) re-uses an already-persisted `entry.modelProvider`. After this fix that value is canonical for any new run; legacy stored sessions remain unchanged. No migration is shipped — stale entries naturally update on the next non-preserve run.
- Other consumers of `sessionInfo.modelProvider` (e.g. UI controls) are unaffected because the field's contract becomes *more* aligned with what they already render.

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:** TUI footer shows runtime id (`claude-cli/...`) instead of canonical provider (`anthropic/...`) when a model is routed through a CLI backend.

**Real environment tested:** macOS, source repo `/Users/rob/Development/openclaw`, branch `feature/canonical-modelprovider`. User config has `anthropic/claude-*` pinned to `agentRuntime.id = "claude-cli"`.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

```
pnpm exec vitest run src/agents/command/session-store.test.ts
pnpm tsgo:core
```

**Evidence after fix:** Terminal output, real run:

```
Test Files  2 passed (2)
      Tests  74 passed (74)
```

`pnpm tsgo:core` exit=0.

The new test `canonicalizes CLI runtime-id provider before persisting modelProvider` was first written failing (`Expected: "anthropic" Received: "claude-cli"`) and then made green with the helper-based fix — confirming both the root cause and the resolution.

**Observed result after fix:** `sessionInfo.modelProvider` now persists the canonical provider id. With the same TUI footer renderer (`${modelProvider}/${model}`), the status line will display `anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-6` instead of `claude-cli/claude-sonnet-4-6`.

**What was not tested:**

- Live TUI footer render against this source build (requires installing the source dist; deferred to user smoke after merge or via `pnpm gateway:dev`).
- Migration of pre-existing session-store entries that carry the old runtime-id format (none performed; legacy values heal on next run).

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

target.title: fix(compact): make /compact command cancelable via abortEmbeddedAgentRun
target.body:
## Summary

Fix #66535 for OpenClaw-owned `/compact` execution: a manual context-engine compaction now becomes an abortable active embedded run while the queued compaction task is actually running, so `/stop` can find it and abort the signal passed into the compaction engine.

Changes:
- Register a synthetic active embedded-run handle inside the queued context-engine compaction task, not at command entry time.
- Scope that synthetic active-run registration to standalone manual compaction only when no active run is already registered, so budget/preflight compaction cannot replace the real active run handle.
- Forward caller aborts through the queued compaction signal and pass that signal to `compactContextEngineWithSafetyTimeout`.
- Always clear the synthetic handle in `finally` after queued compaction exits.
- Refuse to start a replacement `/compact` if an existing abortable run does not drain within 15s.

Codex-native app-server compaction is intentionally not claimed as fixed here. Codex `thread/compact/start` returns immediately and real server-side cancellation requires `turn/interrupt(threadId, turnId)`, so OpenClaw should not keep a stale active-run handle for Codex-native pending compaction without a real Codex turn lifecycle owner.

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed:**
Before: `/compact` could abort an existing compaction-capable run, but the new manual context-engine compaction was not registered as an active run at the execution boundary, so `/stop` could miss it or a command-level handle could race queue ordering.
After: queued context-engine compaction registers an active handle only while the queued task is executing. `/stop` aborts that handle, the engine receives an aborted `AbortSignal`, and cleanup clears the active run after completion.

**Real environment tested:**
Local Linux checkout, Node via repo test wrapper on branch `fix/66535-compact-cancelable-refresh` at commit `39f6298bc9e17b2c3885476eb5967ec99dca40c7`.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

```bash
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/auto-reply/reply/commands-compact.test.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/compact.hooks.test.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/runs.test.ts

node_modules/.bin/oxfmt --check src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/compact.queued.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/compact.hooks.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/commands-compact.ts src/auto-reply/reply/commands-compact.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/commands-compact.runtime.ts src/agents/embedded-agent.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-runner.ts
git diff --check
.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode branch --base upstream/main
```

**Evidence after fix:**

```text

Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests  14 passed (14)
Test Files  2 passed (2)
Tests  95 passed (95)
[test] passed 2 Vitest shards in 34.95s
```

New/updated focused assertions include:
- `registers queued context-engine compaction as an abortable active run`
- `forwards the caller abort signal into the engine compact() call`
- `does not replace an active run when abort drain times out`
- `does not replace an existing active run handle during budget compaction`

```text
# passed with no findings
```

```text
All matched files use the correct format.
```

```text
git diff --check
# passed
```

```text
autoreview clean: no accepted/actionable findings reported
overall: patch is correct (0.78)
```

**Observed result after fix:**
- Queued manual context-engine `/compact` exposes an active embedded-run handle only during the queued work and only when no real active run is already registered.
- `abortEmbeddedAgentRun(sessionId)` aborts the compaction signal while compaction is in flight.
- The synthetic active handle is cleared after compaction exits; budget/preflight compaction preserves any existing real active run handle.
- `/compact` no longer replaces a still-draining abortable run after a drain timeout.

**What was not tested:**
- Live gateway with a real LLM-backed `/compact` followed by `/stop`.
- Codex app-server native compaction cancellation via `turn/interrupt(threadId, turnId)`; this PR avoids claiming that behavior.

## Production function proof

Called the actual production functions from source via `node --import tsx` to verify active-run handle lifecycle:

```bash

};

console.log('Before registration:     isActive:', isEmbeddedAgentRunActive(sessionId));

console.log('After registration:      isActive:', isEmbeddedAgentRunActive(sessionId));
console.log('abortEmbeddedAgentRun:   aborted:', abortEmbeddedAgentRun(sessionId));
clearActiveEmbeddedRun(sessionId, handle, 'proof-key', 'proof-file');
console.log('After clear:             isActive:', isEmbeddedAgentRunActive(sessionId));
console.log('Abort nonexistent run:   aborted:', abortEmbeddedAgentRun('__nonexistent__'));
"
```

target.title: fix(telegram): route outbound sends through configured default account (#61012)
target.body:
## Summary

With multiple Telegram bot accounts configured, outbound sends via `openclaw message send --channel telegram` (and proactive/subagent sends) ignored the configured default account and always routed through the implicit env/top-level `"default"` bot. This makes the outbound account resolver honor the configured default when no account is specified, so:

1. `message send --channel telegram` with **no** `--account` uses the configured default bot.
2. `--account <id>` routes to that bot.
3. Subagent/proactive sends (which omit `--account`) use the correct bot identity.

## Root cause

`extensions/telegram/src/accounts.ts` → `resolveTelegramAccount()` passed the raw (possibly `undefined`) `accountId` straight into the shared `resolveAccountWithDefaultFallback()` helper:

```ts
return resolveAccountWithDefaultFallback({
  accountId: params.accountId, // undefined when no --account
  ...
});
```

When `accountId` is omitted, the helper normalizes `undefined` → the literal `"default"` account id and resolves that **first**. If the implicit `"default"` account has any credential — e.g. a top-level `channels.telegram.botToken` or `TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN` left over from a single-bot setup — the helper returns it immediately and never consults `resolveDefaultTelegramAccountId()`. So `channels.telegram.defaultAccount` (and the default-agent binding) was silently ignored and every send went out under the `"default"` bot. The helper's `resolveDefaultAccountId` fallback only fires when the `"default"` account has *no* credential — precisely the case where the bug doesn't bite.

The outbound path confirms this is the deciding point: the durable send adapter (`outbound-adapter.ts`) calls `sendMessageTelegram(to, text, { cfg, accountId })` with **no** explicit token, so `resolveTelegramAccount()` alone decides the bot/token.

The sibling channel `extensions/nextcloud-talk/src/accounts.ts` already does this correctly — it pre-resolves `params.accountId ?? resolveDefaultNextcloudTalkAccountId(cfg)` before calling the same helper.

## The fix

Mirror the nextcloud-talk resolver: resolve the configured default account id up front when none is provided, then pass that into the helper.

```ts
const resolvedAccountId = params.accountId ?? resolveDefaultTelegramAccountId(params.cfg);
return resolveAccountWithDefaultFallback({
  accountId: resolvedAccountId,
  ...
});
```

`resolveDefaultTelegramAccountId()` already encapsulates the correct precedence (default-agent binding → `channels.telegram.defaultAccount` → first listed / implicit `"default"`), so single-bot, env-only, explicit `--account`, and inbound routing are unchanged; only the previously-broken multi-bot default selection changes.

Scope note: `inspectTelegramAccount()` uses the same helper for capability/status discovery (not send routing). Its omitted-`accountId` callers either branch to the all-accounts union (`channel-actions.ts`) or are diagnostic, so they don't affect which bot sends; left out of this bug-scoped change.

## Verification

Done in an isolated linked worktree on `fix/issue-61012-telegram-default-account` (based on latest `upstream/main`). **OpenClaw was not run locally** — no gateway/CLI/`pnpm dev`/`openclaw …`. Verified by code review + focused Vitest unit tests only.

Added a focused colocated test in `extensions/telegram/src/accounts.test.ts` covering default-account selection (no flag) and explicit-account selection, and confirmed it fails before the fix and passes after:

- **Before** (fix one-liner reverted): `routes omitted-account sends through the configured defaultAccount (#61012)` fails — `AssertionError: expected 'default' to be 'secondary'` (1 failed | 35 passed).
- **After**: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/telegram/src/accounts.test.ts` → **36 passed**.
- **Regression** (send/account/outbound path): `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs` over `send.test.ts action-runtime.test.ts outbound-adapter.test.ts bot-message-dispatch.test.ts channel.gateway.test.ts channel.message-adapter.test.ts send.proxy.test.ts` → **345 passed (7 files)**.

Not tested: live multi-bot Telegram delivery (no real bots/tokens exercised). No tokens or secrets are included in the code, tests, or this PR.

Fixes #61012

target.title: feat(plugins): expose compaction summary with hook opt-in

Fixes #61447.

## Summary
- Adds optional `summary` to `after_compaction` hook payloads when compaction produced summary text.
- Gates summary delivery behind the existing `plugins.entries.<id>.hooks.allowConversationAccess` policy.
- Keeps default/non-trusted `after_compaction` handlers on metadata-only payloads.
- Documents `after_compaction.summary` as transcript-derived sensitive content.

## Notes
This intentionally does not expose summaries by default, so metadata-only compaction observers keep the current behavior and summary access remains aligned with the existing conversation-access trust boundary.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: `after_compaction` can now carry compaction summary text, while untrusted/default plugin hooks still receive only metadata.
- Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw worktree on Linux, running this PR branch with the actual `src/plugins/hooks.ts` hook runner through `node --import tsx`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: Ran a local Node proof that imports `createHookRunner`, registers two real `after_compaction` handlers, and calls `runner.runAfterCompaction(...)` with a payload containing `summary: "redacted local proof summary"`.

- What was not tested: I did not run a full live Gateway conversation compaction against a real chat account for this PR.

target.title: chore(codeowners): gate heartbeat template and repair changes
target.body:
## Summary

Add CODEOWNERS review gates for heartbeat template, content emptiness check, template resolution, and doctor repair logic. Changes to any of these files now require review from @hxy91819 or @vincentkoc.

### Scope

| File | Role | Risk if changed incorrectly |
|---|---|---|
| `src/agents/templates/HEARTBEAT.md` | Runtime heartbeat template content | Non-comment lines in template cause heartbeat API calls to fire for all newly bootstrapped workspaces |
| `src/agents/workspace-templates.ts` | Template directory resolution and search paths | Changes to `FALLBACK_TEMPLATE_DIR` or candidate order could redirect heartbeat template loading back to docs directory, breaking the decoupling from #85416 |
| `src/auto-reply/heartbeat.ts` | `isHeartbeatContentEffectivelyEmpty()` and `parseHeartbeatTasks()` | Changes to emptiness check logic can cause false skips (heartbeat not firing) or false triggers (unnecessary API calls) |
| `src/commands/doctor-heartbeat-template-repair.ts` | Doctor repair matching and fix logic | Hardcoded constants are coupled to template content; mismatches cause missed detections or false `dirty-template-with-custom-content` verdicts |

### Background

Historically, the heartbeat runtime template (`HEARTBEAT.md`) was loaded from the docs template directory (`docs/reference/templates/`). Docs edits (frontmatter, fenced code blocks, prose descriptions, "Related" sections) leaked into user workspace files after `stripFrontMatter`, causing:

- Non-comment/non-header lines (prose, markdown links like `- [Heartbeat config](...)`) made `isHeartbeatContentEffectivelyEmpty()` return `false`, triggering heartbeat API calls for users who never configured any tasks.
- This happened multiple times across different docs template versions (2026-01, 2026-03, 2026-04), resulting in numerous user complaints about unexpected heartbeat activity and API token consumption.

PR #85416 decoupled the runtime template to `src/agents/templates/HEARTBEAT.md`, preventing docs edits from affecting runtime behavior. However, the risk remains: if any of the gated files are modified incorrectly, the same class of regression reoccurs.

### Purpose

The purpose of this CODEOWNERS gate is to ensure that any change to these files triggers a required review discussion. A seemingly harmless edit can cause all newly bootstrapped workspaces to fire heartbeat API calls unexpectedly, leading to significant unintended token consumption for users. The designated reviewers can evaluate whether the change is safe and whether the coupled code (doctor repair constants, emptiness check logic, template resolution paths) needs to be updated in tandem.

## Verification

- `git diff --check`
- CODEOWNERS syntax verified against GitHub documentation (last-match-wins semantics, user/team references)
